rules.name: Missing Authorization Check
rules.description: Functions or endpoints that perform sensitive operations without verifying the caller has the required role or permission. Attackers can access resources or actions belonging to other users.
rules.remediation_summary: Enforce access control on every route that reads, modifies, or deletes data. Use a centralised authorisation layer (e.g., RBAC/ABAC middleware). Deny by default and validate that the authenticated user owns or is permitted to access the requested resource.

rules.name: Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR)
rules.description: User-supplied identifiers (IDs, filenames, UUIDs) are used directly to fetch records without confirming the requesting user is authorised to access that object.
rules.remediation_summary: After fetching the object, assert that the current user is its owner or has explicit permission. Prefer opaque, unpredictable identifiers (UUIDs v4) and always pair with an ownership check before returning or mutating the record.

rules.name: Directory Traversal
rules.description: Unvalidated user input is concatenated into file paths, allowing an attacker to escape the intended directory and read or write arbitrary files on the server.
rules.remediation_summary: Resolve the canonical path and assert it starts with the expected base directory before opening any file. Use allowlists of permitted filenames rather than accepting arbitrary user-supplied paths. Consider serving user-uploaded files through a dedicated storage service.

rules.name: Weak or Broken Hash Algorithm for Passwords
rules.description: Passwords stored using MD5, SHA-1, or unsalted SHA-256 are trivially crackable with rainbow tables or GPU brute force. These algorithms were designed for speed, not password storage.
rules.remediation_summary: Use a purpose-built, slow password hashing function: bcrypt (cost ≥ 12), scrypt, or Argon2id. Never implement your own salting. Migrate existing MD5/SHA-1 hashes by re-hashing on next successful login.

rules.name: Hardcoded Secret or Credential
rules.description: API keys, passwords, tokens, or private keys embedded directly in source code are exposed to anyone with repository access and persist in git history even after deletion.
rules.remediation_summary: Move all secrets to environment variables or a secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, Azure Key Vault). Rotate any credential that has already been committed. Add a pre-commit hook (e.g., detect-secrets or trufflehog) to prevent future occurrences.

rules.name: Sensitive Data Transmitted Without Encryption
rules.description: Sensitive data such as credentials, PII, or tokens sent over HTTP (not HTTPS) can be intercepted by a network-level attacker.
rules.remediation_summary: Enforce TLS 1.2+ for all network communication. Never disable certificate verification in production. Use HSTS headers and redirect all HTTP traffic to HTTPS.

rules.name: SQL Injection via String Concatenation
rules.description: User-supplied input concatenated directly into a SQL query string allows an attacker to alter query logic, dump data, or execute commands against the database.
rules.remediation_summary: Use parameterised queries or prepared statements exclusively. Never interpolate user input into query strings. Apply an ORM with built-in parameter binding. Limit database user privileges to the minimum required (principle of least privilege).

rules.name: OS Command Injection
rules.description: Unsanitised user input passed to shell execution functions enables an attacker to run arbitrary commands on the host operating system.
rules.remediation_summary: Never pass user input to shell execution functions. Prefer library-based alternatives to shelling out. If a subprocess is unavoidable, pass arguments as a list (never shell=True) and validate input against a strict allowlist before use.

rules.name: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) - Reflected or Stored
rules.description: Unescaped user-supplied content rendered in HTML responses lets an attacker inject scripts that execute in victims' browsers, enabling session hijacking or credential theft.
rules.remediation_summary: Auto-escape all template output by default. Never use | safe or Markup() on user input. Apply a strict Content-Security-Policy header. Validate and sanitise HTML input with an allowlist library (e.g., bleach, DOMPurify) when rich text is required.

rules.name: Debug Mode Enabled in Production
rules.description: Running a web framework with debug mode active exposes interactive error pages, stack traces, and sometimes a remote code execution console to anyone who can trigger an error.
rules.remediation_summary: Set DEBUG = False and configure error handling to return generic error pages in production. Use environment variables to toggle debug settings so they cannot be accidentally committed. Verify the production config in CI before deployment.

rules.name: Overly Permissive CORS Policy
rules.description: Setting Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * or reflecting the request Origin without validation allows any website to make credentialed cross-origin requests to the API.
rules.remediation_summary: Define an explicit allowlist of trusted origins. Never combine Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true with a wildcard origin. Validate the Origin header against the allowlist and only reflect it when it matches.

rules.name: Missing Security Headers
rules.description: Absence of HTTP security headers such as Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options, and Strict-Transport-Security leaves the application vulnerable to clickjacking, MIME sniffing, and protocol downgrade attacks.
rules.remediation_summary: Add a security-header middleware (e.g., Flask-Talisman, Helmet.js) to every response. At minimum include: Content-Security-Policy, Strict-Transport-Security, X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, X-Frame-Options: DENY, and Referrer-Policy.

rules.name: Missing Brute-Force Protection on Login
rules.description: Login endpoints without rate limiting or account lockout allow unlimited password guessing attempts, making credential stuffing and brute-force attacks trivial.
rules.remediation_summary: Implement progressive rate limiting on authentication endpoints (e.g., flask-limiter, express-rate-limit). Lock accounts temporarily after N consecutive failures. Use CAPTCHA for repeated failures from the same IP. Consider time-based one-time passwords (TOTP) as a second factor.

rules.name: Weak or Insecure Session Configuration
rules.description: Session cookies without HttpOnly, Secure, or SameSite flags, or with excessively long expiry times, can be stolen via XSS or CSRF attacks.
rules.remediation_summary: Set HttpOnly=True, Secure=True, and SameSite=Strict (or Lax) on all session cookies. Keep session lifetime short (≤ 30 minutes idle). Regenerate session IDs after login. Invalidate sessions on the server side at logout.

rules.name: JWT Secret Key Weakness or Algorithm Confusion
rules.description: Using a weak, hardcoded, or default JWT secret, or accepting the 'none' algorithm, allows attackers to forge tokens and impersonate any user.
rules.remediation_summary: Sign JWTs with a cryptographically random secret of at least 256 bits (or an RSA/EC private key for asymmetric signing). Explicitly specify the expected algorithm on decode and reject 'none'. Store the secret in an environment variable or secrets manager, not in code.

rules.owasp_category: A09:2021 - Security Logging and Monitoring Failures
rules.name: Authentication Events Not Logged
rules.description: Failed and successful login attempts, password resets, and account lockouts that are not logged make it impossible to detect credential-stuffing campaigns or account takeover attempts.
rules.remediation_summary: Log every authentication event (success, failure, lockout) with timestamp, username (not password), source IP, and user-agent. Ship logs to a centralised SIEM. Set up alerts for thresholds like > 10 failures per minute from a single IP. Retain logs for at least 90 days.

rules.owasp_category: A09:2021 - Security Logging and Monitoring Failures
rules.name: Sensitive Data in Log Output
rules.description: Logging passwords, tokens, credit card numbers, or other PII creates a secondary exposure vector. Log aggregation systems often have weaker access controls than the primary datastore.
rules.remediation_summary: Scrub or mask sensitive fields before logging. Use a log sanitisation library or a custom filter that redacts fields matching known-sensitive keys (password, token, ssn, card). Never log full request bodies at INFO or higher in production. Apply structured logging so fields can be filtered programmatically.

rules.owasp_category: A09:2021 - Security Logging and Monitoring Failures
rules.name: Missing Audit Trail for Privileged Operations
rules.description: Administrative or high-privilege actions (user deletion, role changes, config updates, data exports) performed without an audit log prevent post-incident forensics and compliance reporting.
rules.remediation_summary: Write an immutable audit log entry for every privileged operation recording: who performed it, what action was taken, on which resource, and when. Consider append-only storage for audit logs. Integrate with your SIEM and create alerts for unexpected privilege escalations.
